graph.name: Incident Response: INC-TEST-001
graph.description: Full incident response: triage -> diagnose -> contain -> remediate

incident.start.context: Assess the severity and immediate impact of this incident.

active_impact.hint: Is there ongoing impact right now?

incident.observe.context: Gather all observable facts about the problem. Do not jump to conclusions.

affected_systems.hint: What systems or components are affected?

incident.hypothesize.prompt: Form a hypothesis about the root cause.
incident.hypothesize.context: Based on your observations, what do you think is causing the problem?

hypothesis.hint: Your best explanation for the observed symptoms

incident.test.context: Design and execute a test that would confirm or refute your hypothesis.

incident.contain.context: Take immediate action to stop ongoing damage or impact.

incident.remediate.context: Apply a fix for the root cause identified during diagnosis.

rollback_plan.hint: How to roll back if the fix fails?

incident.conclude.context: Based on all evidence gathered, what is the root cause?

incident.verify.context: Confirm the incident is fully resolved and services are restored.

incident.start.objective: Production database connection pool exhaustion causing intermittent 503 errors for end users

incident.observe.symptoms: Intermittent HTTP 503 errors returned to approximately 15% of user requests. Application logs show 'connection pool exhausted' errors from the ORM layer. Database server CPU and memory appear normal. Connection count on the database server shows 500 connections (max configured is 500).
incident.observe.onset: 2026-03-05T08:30:00Z - first alerts triggered at 08:32 UTC. User reports began arriving at 08:35 UTC.
incident.observe.affected_systems: Primary web application (api.example.com), user-facing dashboard, mobile app backend. Internal admin panel is unaffected (uses separate connection pool).

incident.hypothesize.hypothesis: A recent deployment (v2.14.3, deployed at 08:15 UTC) introduced a code path that opens database connections without properly closing them. The connection leak gradually exhausted the pool over ~15 minutes, causing the 503 errors when new requests could not acquire connections.
incident.hypothesize.alternative_hypotheses: 1) A spike in legitimate traffic exceeded pool capacity (ruled less likely since traffic volume is normal). 2) The database server itself became slow, causing connections to be held longer than normal (less likely since DB CPU/memory are normal). 3) A background job or cron task is holding connections open (possible but timing correlates with deployment).

incident.test.test_description: 1) Compared git diff between v2.14.2 and v2.14.3 focusing on database access patterns. Found a new endpoint /api/v2/reports/export that opens a cursor but lacks a finally block to close it. 2) Checked connection pool metrics over time: connections climbed linearly from 200 to 500 between 08:15 and 08:30, plateauing at max. 3) Reproduced in staging: hitting the /reports/export endpoint 100 times leaked 100 connections.
incident.test.test_result: Confirmed: the /api/v2/reports/export endpoint in v2.14.3 leaks one database connection per request. The staging reproduction showed identical behavior. Connection count rises monotonically without recovery.

incident.contain.containment_action: Rolled back production deployment from v2.14.3 to v2.14.2 at 09:15 UTC. After rollback, manually killed the 300 leaked idle connections on the database server to free the pool. Monitoring confirmed connection count dropped from 500 to ~180 (normal baseline) within 2 minutes.

incident.remediate.fix_description: Added proper connection lifecycle management to /api/v2/reports/export: wrapped the database cursor in a context manager (with db.connection() as conn:) to guarantee connection release on both success and exception paths. Added a unit test that verifies connection count returns to baseline after calling the endpoint. Fix committed as v2.14.4-hotfix.
incident.remediate.rollback_plan: If v2.14.4-hotfix causes issues, roll back to v2.14.2 (the last known-good version). The /reports/export feature will be unavailable but all other functionality is unaffected. Rollback command: kubectl rollout undo deployment/api-server

incident.conclude.root_cause: A database connection leak in the /api/v2/reports/export endpoint introduced in v2.14.3. The endpoint opened a database cursor without a context manager or finally block, causing every request to leak one connection. Over ~15 minutes of normal traffic, this exhausted the 500-connection pool.
incident.conclude.evidence_summary: 1) Code diff confirmed missing connection close in v2.14.3. 2) Connection pool metrics showed linear climb from 200 to 500 connections between 08:15 (deploy time) and 08:30 (first alerts). 3) Staging reproduction confirmed 1:1 leak ratio (100 requests = 100 leaked connections). 4) Rollback to v2.14.2 immediately resolved the issue. 5) Hotfix v2.14.4 with context manager verified to not leak connections.
incident.conclude.recommended_action: 1) Deploy v2.14.4-hotfix to production after verification. 2) Add connection pool monitoring alerts for when usage exceeds 80% of max. 3) Add a code review checklist item for database connection lifecycle management. 4) Consider adding a connection pool leak detector to the CI/CD pipeline.

incident.verify.verification_notes: v2.14.4-hotfix deployed to production at 10:45 UTC. Connection pool stable at ~180 connections for 30 minutes post-deploy. Zero 503 errors in the monitoring dashboard since rollback at 09:15 UTC. The /reports/export endpoint tested in production and confirmed functional with proper connection release. All user-facing services (API, dashboard, mobile backend) operating normally.

incident.close.summary: Database connection pool exhaustion caused by a connection leak in the /api/v2/reports/export endpoint introduced in v2.14.3. Impact lasted approximately 45 minutes (08:30-09:15 UTC) affecting ~15% of user requests with 503 errors. Root cause identified via code diff and staging reproduction. Contained by rolling back to v2.14.2. Permanently fixed in v2.14.4-hotfix with proper context manager usage. All services restored and verified stable.
